body: On regards of food on campus, it sucks! Is the thing that I dislike the most about the UoA. The lack of a proper dinning ball at UoA. Rutgers (where I did my BS), has 4 different dinning halls, and there are all buffet style, RU also has cafeterias in different part of campus where you can use your meal plans. Obviously Rutgers is much bigger than the UoA but having at least 1 proper dinning hall with all you can eat will be awesome.

title: University of Arizona - 5 Things to Avoid

author: @losthighway4840

title: University of Arizona - 5 Things to Avoid
body: U of A alum from back in the 90s, in the days before students brought cell phones and laptops to class. 1) If I'm not mistaken, sometimes large lectures and 8am classes are unavoidable depending on your major. I did better in those massive general ed lecture classes sometimes than I did in some of the courses in my major! If you have to be on Facebook during a lecture, you're not a serious student and I'm not gonna cry for you when you get a bad grade on your midterm! 2) Living within 1-2 miles of campus is absolutely best, and housing options for students in the surrounding neighborhoods have doubled since my years as a student. Tucson has always had a decent bus system and now there's the streetcar, which I wish they would have had in the 90s, because it's awesome! 3) Absolutely agree about doing most of your food shopping and eating off campus, it WILL save you money. 4) Tucson is just as much a retirement town as it is a college town, and a lot of snowbirds are U of A alumni who retire to Tucson and come back for the sporting events and Homecoming - I plan to be one of those old people! 5) Don't party your way through college. Take your academics seriously. Your future employers - and employees - will thank you 20 years from now.
author: @micmac99

title: Arizona State University Review | Worth the Money for Busy Adults?

author: @T-Michaels

title: Arizona State University Review | Worth the Money for Busy Adults?
body:
I just graduated from ASU a few weeks back. I'm in the military, so I have a bit of a different perspective, but overall, I'm really happy with my experience there. They gave me a lot of credits for military experience, waive fees beyond TA, and have a great support network for active duty and veterans. It was more time consuming than degrees some of my friends are doing at AMU/WGU, but I think it'll be worth it on the back end. I've already started a master's program at Johns Hopkins, and I'm not sure I would've gotten in had I gone to one of those other schools.

Good luck and fork 'em!
author: @codywilson9506

title: Arizona State University Review | Worth the Money for Busy Adults?
body: Fun fact… ASU online partners with Uber for tuition free education. You have to complete 2,000 rides and maintain 200 points to keep your scholarship! Hence why I’m looking at this video!

title: Arizona State University Review | Worth the Money for Busy Adults?
body: One thing that can make ASU more affordable is to complete an associate degree through Rio Salado College (an online community college that partners with ASU for transfer credit). Rio Salado accepts CLEP,  some DSST tests, and Straighterline credits.

title: Arizona State University Review | Worth the Money for Busy Adults?
body: Before transfering to ASU Online (from another online school) they had me take their "DARS" audit to see which of my courses would apply to my major and I'm to say that they all transferred... even if most of them only transferred as an elective. I've been doing ASU online now for the last 10 months and most profressors are pretty hands off, it's very predicitble deadlines (which I love) and the cost is next to zero because I am doing the Uber Partnership benefit. But thank you for your review, it was also helpful!
